findings.evidence: validator.py is byte-for-byte identical between base and head. The regex _EMAIL_RE = re.compile(r"^[A-Za-z0-9.\-]+@[A-Za-z0-9.\-]+\.[A-Za-z]{2,}$") still has no '+' in the local-part character class, so 'a+b@example.com' still returns False. The PR claims the fix was applied; the code proves it was not.
findings.why_tests_missed_it: The new test (test_validator.py:18) asserts 'result in (True, False)', which is a tautology — it passes whether validate_email returns True or False. Running the new test against the unmodified base code also produces a green result, so CI gives a false pass.
findings.fix: Add '+' to the local-part character class in _EMAIL_RE, e.g. r"^[A-Za-z0-9.\-+]+@[A-Za-z0-9.\-]+\.[A-Za-z]{2,}$", and replace the tautological assertion with 'assert validate_email("a+b@example.com") is True'.

findings.evidence: test_plus_addressing_supported() asserts 'result in (True, False)'. Every boolean value satisfies this condition; the assertion can never fail. It provides zero signal about whether the claimed fix works.
findings.why_tests_missed_it: The assertion itself is the masking mechanism: a vacuous predicate that accepts any return value, so the test is structurally incapable of detecting the unfixed behaviour.
findings.fix: Replace with 'assert result is True' (after actually fixing the regex) so the test would have been red on the base code.
